story:
Each morning I drive the longer way to work. It adds a mile and crawls with traffic, but it’s my ritual—my link to a moment of joy. I first found that route by accident: lost on the way to my new job, anxious about being late, I pulled into a grocery store to ask for directions to Barley Street. The cashier, a woman near the entrance, kindly drew me a verbal map, and then we kept talking. As a single man, I glanced at her left hand; at the same time, she glanced at mine. She asked if I’d be alone for dinner. I said yes. That night became our first date. More dates followed. We married.

For four years, I thought we were wonderful. She must have felt differently: she filed for divorce and soon married someone who worked where I did. I wondered if she had started noticing him at our office parties. My male coworkers ask why I still drive the long way past that store. I tell them it marks a place of joy I can’t quite let go of.

I’m a man of rituals. Before every Toronto Maple Leafs game, I put on the same blue shirt my mother bought me when I was twelve. They haven’t won the Stanley Cup in my lifetime. The shirt is tight now, but I still wear it. Sometimes I joke that maybe the ritual should be wearing skates in the living room instead.

A year after the divorce, I began to think I should give up the driving ritual. I catch myself turning my head away from the grocery store as I pass. Then one morning I overslept—twenty minutes gone—after staying up for a late Leafs game in Los Angeles that went to overtime. I’d argued with myself at the end of regulation whether to keep watching, and I did, to prove I was loyal. They lost anyway. So much for loyalty. Running late, I finally took the faster route, the one right along the street where I work.

I left so quickly I forgot my coffee, another ritual. When I spotted a shop called Carol’s Coffee, I pulled in fast enough to make the driver behind me nervous. The barista, who introduced herself as Carol, served me with a genuine smile. I explained I’d rushed out and skipped my coffee. I wanted to talk longer, but I was late, and the man behind me looked equally caffeine-starved. I waved; she smiled again. By the time I reached the door, I’d decided: this would be my new route, and Carol’s would be part of the ritual. If she wasn’t wearing a ring, I might try a careful compliment and see if there was room for conversation—not a plan, exactly, but a hope.

I got to work ten minutes early and held the door for a woman I didn’t recognize. She spoke as if she knew me: “You’re the romantic who takes the long way because you married the woman you met asking for directions—and still do it even after she left, right?” I told her not anymore; I’d taken the shortcut today. She smiled. She said she liked a man who believes in rituals; she had her own. She suggested dinner at a diner on Barley Street—a ritual she used to share with her former husband. “We could share one,” she said. “Who knows what could happen?”

I couldn’t find words. I nodded. My faith in ritual wasn’t lost after all.
